planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; hop tree is one of the few native small trees genuinely adapted to understory shade and is an important larval host plant for the giant swallowtail butterfly — the trifoliate leaves are the primary food source for giant swallowtail caterpillars east of the Rockies.

directions: Plant in moist to moderately dry, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; hop tree is a native small tree or large shrub found naturally in woodland edges, river bluffs, and dry rocky hillsides from southern Ontario to Florida and Texas, highly unusual among small trees in its genuine shade-tolerance — it grows in understory conditions beneath taller trees where most ornamentals fail. The common names derive from its two most notable features: the distinctive, round, wafer-like seed capsules (samaras) that ripen in fall and were historically used as a substitute for hops in brewing, and the compound leaves with three leaflets that carry a strong, distinctive aroma when crushed. Clusters of small, greenish-white flowers in early summer attract many pollinators but are unshowy — the ornamental interest lies in the attractive trifoliate foliage, yellow fall color, and the striking, papery, disc-like fruits that hang in clusters and rattle in winter wind. Most importantly, it is the primary and preferred larval host plant of the giant swallowtail butterfly (Papilio cresphontes), North America's largest butterfly, and a significant host for the tiger swallowtail as well.

planting.note: Root softwood cuttings in early summer or plant container-grown plants in moist, humus-rich, acidic, well-drained soil in part shade; sweet azalea is naturally a streamside and moist woodland plant and requires consistent soil moisture. It blooms 6–8 weeks after most other deciduous azaleas, filling the early-summer gap in the native azalea sequence from May through July.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, consistently moist, acidic soil in part shade; sweet azalea is one of the latest-blooming native deciduous azaleas of eastern North America, native to streambanks, moist woodlands, and cool mountain ravines from Pennsylvania to Alabama, producing its flowers in late June through July — 6–8 weeks after most other azaleas have finished — creating invaluable sequence of color in the native woodland garden. The flowers are exceptional among azaleas for their fragrance: strongly and sweetly scented, with a light, honeysuckle-like fragrance that perfumes the surrounding air, reminiscent of heliotrope or vanilla. The blooms are white to pale blush-pink with prominent, very long red stamens that extend well beyond the petals, giving the open flowers a delicate, spider-like appearance against the green foliage. The foliage is glossy and smooth (giving rise to 'smooth azalea'), turning attractive red and orange in fall. Among native azaleas, it is one of the most adaptable in the garden and one of the most underplanted, despite combining fragrance, late bloom season, and attractive form in a single shade-tolerant shrub.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in well-drained, average to fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Amur maackia tolerates a wide range of soil conditions including clay and is notably pH-adaptable for a legume. As a nitrogen-fixing tree, it improves soil fertility over time and requires little or no supplemental fertilizer once established.

directions: Plant in well-drained, average to fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Amur maackia is a genuinely underappreciated small to medium deciduous tree from the forests of northeastern China and Manchuria, valued primarily for blooming in July and August when almost no other trees are flowering — producing upright, dense racemes of small, white pea flowers with a delicate blue cast that are mildly fragrant and attractive to bees and other pollinators. The tree is a legume and fixes atmospheric nitrogen, gradually improving soil fertility. The foliage emerges in spring with a distinctive silvery-blue sheen as leaves unfurl (a reliable spring identification feature) and matures to dark glossy green through summer. The copper-brown, peeling bark on older specimens is ornamental year-round. Highly adaptable to urban conditions, clay soils, drought, and air pollution, and one of the hardiest flowering trees available (to zone 3). Closely related to Cladrastis (yellowwood) and with a similar leguminous form. An excellent, pest-resistant, four-season small street or garden tree that remains widely unknown outside specialist circles.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in fall in well-drained, lean, slightly acidic soil in full sun to part shade; silk tassel bush is native to the fog-belt coastal chaparral of central and northern California and performs best in cool, moist coastal climates with dry summers. It establishes poorly from transplants in summer heat and drought. The cultivar 'James Roof' produces the longest catkins — up to 14 inches.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean, slightly acidic soil in full sun to part shade; silk tassel bush is a California native evergreen shrub or small tree producing some of the most dramatic winter catkins of any temperate plant — long, silky, pale gray-green to silver catkins that hang in cascading clusters from the branch tips of male plants from December through February, swaying in the winter wind and creating an arresting spectacle during the garden's emptiest period. On the male cultivar 'James Roof' (the most widely grown), these catkins can reach 14 inches long and are present in such abundance that the entire shrub seems draped with silver-gray fringe. Female plants produce grape-like clusters of purple berries if a male pollinator is nearby. The foliage is leathery, wavy-edged, and deep glossy green with woolly gray undersides, giving the shrub excellent texture year-round. Drought-tolerant once established and native to coastal California chaparral and mixed evergreen forest, it thrives in mild climates but resents excessive summer irrigation. An outstanding specimen for dry gardens, woodland edges, and shrub borders where winter interest is most needed.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in moist, humus-rich, consistently moist, acidic, well-drained soil in part shade; disanthus is slow-growing and dislikes root disturbance — plant carefully and do not move. It requires reliably moist, acidic conditions similar to Japanese maples and kalmia, and grows naturally in moist mountain forests of Japan and central China.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, consistently moist, acidic soil in part shade; disanthus is a monotypic genus in the witch-hazel family (Hamamelidaceae) — a single species from mountain forests of Japan and central China that is grown primarily as one of the elite autumn-coloring shrubs. The broadly rounded, heart-shaped leaves closely resemble those of Cercis (redbud) in shape, turning in autumn to some of the most spectacular colors achievable in a garden shrub: brilliant combinations of crimson, orange, purple, and claret, often with all colors simultaneously visible on a single leaf. In October, tiny, deep wine-purple flowers appear directly on the bare and falling-leaf branches — so small and dark that they are easily missed but distinctive and curious up close, with reflexed petals in pairs. A slow-growing, understated but ultimately magnificent shrub for the woodland garden with moist, acidic conditions, outstanding autumn color, and a refined year-round form. Often cited by plantsmen as one of the most desirable garden shrubs, despite remaining little-known outside specialist collections.
